title: Authentication & Authorization
description: Every approach to auth explained — sessions, JWTs, OAuth, API keys, RBAC, and more — with security best practices and real implementations

blocks.heading: Authentication vs Authorization
blocks.body: These two terms sound similar but solve completely different problems. Authentication answers the question 'Who are you?' while authorization answers 'What are you allowed to do?' Every secure application needs both, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes developers make.

Think of a restaurant. Authentication is the bouncer at the door checking your ID to confirm you are who you claim to be. Authorization is the host deciding whether you get a regular table or access to the VIP lounge. The bouncer does not care if you are a VIP -- he just needs to verify your identity. The host does not re-check your ID -- she just needs to know your access level. Same principle applies to web applications: first prove who you are, then the system decides what you can do.

blocks.heading: Authentication vs Authorization
rows: Usually invisible -- happens behind the scenes
Rarely -- your identity stays the same
Entering your password to log in
Only admins can delete user accounts
Bouncer checks your ID at the door
Host seats you at regular table or VIP

blocks.heading: Auth Approaches Overview
blocks.body: There is no single correct way to handle authentication. Each approach has trade-offs in security, complexity, and user experience. The right choice depends on your application type, user base, and security requirements.

cards.title: Session-Based
cards.body: Server stores session state, client gets a cookie. The traditional approach used by most server-rendered apps.

cards.title: JWT Tokens
cards.body: Stateless tokens containing encoded user data. Popular for SPAs and mobile apps that talk to APIs.

cards.title: OAuth 2.0
cards.body: Delegated authorization -- let users log in with Google, GitHub, etc. without sharing passwords.

cards.title: API Keys
cards.body: Simple tokens for server-to-server communication and public API access. Easy but limited.

cards.title: Basic Auth
cards.body: Base64-encoded username and password sent with every request. Simple but only safe over HTTPS.

cards.title: Digest Auth
cards.body: Challenge-response protocol that hashes credentials. More secure than Basic but rarely used today.

cards.title: SAML
cards.body: XML-based SSO protocol common in enterprise environments. Complex but powerful for corporate identity.

cards.title: OpenID Connect
cards.body: Identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Adds standardized user info and ID tokens to OAuth flows.

cards.title: Passkeys / WebAuthn
cards.body: Passwordless authentication using biometrics or hardware keys. The future of auth.

cards.title: Magic Links
cards.body: Passwordless login via email. Send a unique time-limited link -- click it and you are in.

cards.title: Multi-Factor Auth
cards.body: Combine multiple factors -- something you know, have, and are -- for stronger security.

cards.title: Certificate-Based
cards.body: Mutual TLS using client certificates. Common in IoT, banking, and government systems.

blocks.heading: Session-Based Authentication
blocks.body: Session-based auth is the oldest and most straightforward approach. When a user logs in, the server creates a session record (stored in memory, a database, or Redis), generates a unique session ID, and sends it back to the browser as a cookie. On every subsequent request, the browser automatically includes that cookie, and the server looks up the session to identify the user.

blocks.description: Flask session-based authentication with login, session check, and logout
React login form that works with session cookies

blocks.heading: Session-Based Auth: Pros and Cons
rows: Easy to revoke -- just delete the session
Hard to scale horizontally without shared session store
Vulnerable to CSRF attacks without protection
No token management on the client
Not ideal for mobile apps or third-party APIs
Built-in support in every web framework
Cookie size limits and cross-domain complexity

blocks.title: Scaling Sessions
blocks.body: Sessions are stateful -- the server must store session data. This makes horizontal scaling harder without sticky sessions or a shared session store like Redis. If you have multiple server instances behind a load balancer, a user's session created on Server A will not exist on Server B unless you share session storage.

blocks.heading: JWT (JSON Web Tokens)
blocks.body: JWTs are self-contained tokens that encode user information directly into the token itself. A JWT has three parts separated by dots: a header (algorithm and type), a payload (user data and claims), and a signature (cryptographic proof the token was not tampered with). Because all the information lives in the token, the server does not need to store session state -- it just verifies the signature.

blocks.description: Flask JWT implementation with token generation, verification middleware, and protected routes
Axios interceptor that attaches JWT to every request and auto-refreshes expired tokens

blocks.body: The simplest JWT approach: issue a single access token on login and include it in every request. When it expires, the user must log in again. This works for short-lived sessions or internal tools where re-authentication is acceptable.

blocks.description: Single access token pattern -- simple but requires re-login on expiry

blocks.body: The production pattern: a short-lived access token (15 minutes) for API requests, plus a long-lived refresh token (7 days) stored in an httpOnly cookie. When the access token expires, the client silently gets a new one using the refresh token. The user stays logged in without re-entering credentials.

blocks.description: Access plus refresh token pattern with token rotation for security
blocks.code:
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
import jwt
import secrets

JWT_SECRET = 'your-secret-key'
refresh_token_store = {}  # In production, use Redis or a database


def create_token_pair(user_id: int, email: str) -> dict:
    access_payload = {
        'sub': user_id,
        'email': email,
        'exp': datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(minutes=15),
        'iat': datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        'type': 'access',
    }
    access_token = jwt.encode(access_payload, JWT_SECRET, algorithm='HS256')

    refresh_id = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    refresh_payload = {
        'sub': user_id,
        'jti': refresh_id,
        'exp': datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=7),
        'iat': datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        'type': 'refresh',
    }
    refresh_token = jwt.encode(refresh_payload, JWT_SECRET, algorithm='HS256')

    # Store refresh token ID for revocation
    refresh_token_store[refresh_id] = {
        'user_id': user_id,
        'expires': datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=7),
    }

    return {'access_token': access_token, 'refresh_token': refresh_token}


def rotate_refresh_token(old_refresh_token: str) -> dict:
    """Verify old refresh token, revoke it, issue new pair."""
    payload = jwt.decode(old_refresh_token, JWT_SECRET, algorithms=['HS256'])

    if payload.get('type') != 'refresh':
        raise ValueError('Not a refresh token')

    jti = payload['jti']
    if jti not in refresh_token_store:
        raise ValueError('Refresh token revoked or unknown')

    # Revoke old token (rotation)
    del refresh_token_store[jti]

    # Issue new pair
    return create_token_pair(payload['sub'], '')

blocks.heading: JWT Pros and Cons
rows: Stateless -- no server-side session storage needed
Cannot be revoked without extra infrastructure (blocklist)
Larger than session cookies (especially with many claims)
Vulnerable to XSS if stored in localStorage
Token expiry requires refresh token logic
Easy to pass to mobile apps and third-party clients
Sensitive data in payload is only encoded, not encrypted

blocks.title: JWT Storage Security
blocks.body: NEVER store JWTs in localStorage for sensitive applications -- it is vulnerable to XSS attacks. Any JavaScript running on your page (including malicious scripts from a supply-chain attack) can read localStorage. Instead, store access tokens in memory (a JavaScript variable) and refresh tokens in httpOnly cookies that JavaScript cannot access.

blocks.heading: Standard JWT Claims (RFC 7519)
rows: Who created and signed the token
Who the token is about (usually user ID)
Who the token is intended for
Token is not valid before this time
Unique identifier to prevent replay attacks

blocks.body: You can also add custom claims like 'role', 'email', or 'permissions'. Keep the payload small -- every claim increases the token size, and the token is sent with every request. Never put sensitive data (passwords, credit card numbers) in JWT claims because the payload is only base64-encoded, not encrypted.

blocks.heading: OAuth 2.0 and Social Login
blocks.body: OAuth 2.0 is a delegated authorization framework. Instead of your app collecting and storing user passwords, you let a trusted provider (Google, GitHub, etc.) handle authentication. The user grants your app limited access to their data on that provider. OAuth 2.0 does not authenticate users directly -- that is what OpenID Connect adds on top.

cards.title: Authorization Code
cards.body: The standard flow for web apps with a backend. Exchanges a short-lived code for tokens server-side. Most secure for traditional web apps.

cards.title: Authorization Code + PKCE
cards.body: Same as Authorization Code but with an extra proof key. Required for SPAs and mobile apps that cannot safely store a client secret.

cards.title: Client Credentials
cards.body: Machine-to-machine auth. No user involved. The client authenticates directly with its own credentials to access its own resources.

cards.title: Device Code
cards.body: For devices with limited input (smart TVs, CLI tools). User authorizes on a separate device by entering a short code.

blocks.title: Redirect to Provider
blocks.body: Your app redirects the user to the OAuth provider's authorization endpoint with your client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and a random state parameter for CSRF protection.

blocks.title: User Approves
blocks.body: The user logs into the provider (if not already) and sees a consent screen listing what permissions your app is requesting. They click 'Allow' to grant access.

blocks.title: Provider Redirects with Code
blocks.body: The provider redirects the user back to your redirect_uri with a short-lived authorization code and the state parameter. You verify the state matches to prevent CSRF.

blocks.title: Exchange Code for Token
blocks.body: Your backend sends the authorization code plus your client_secret to the provider's token endpoint. The provider returns an access token (and optionally a refresh token and ID token).

blocks.title: Use Token to Get User Info
blocks.body: Your backend uses the access token to call the provider's user info endpoint. You get the user's profile data (email, name, avatar) and either create a new account or log in an existing one.

blocks.description: Flask OAuth 2.0 with Google -- redirect and callback endpoints
blocks.code:
from flask import Flask, redirect, request, session, jsonify, url_for
import requests
import os
import secrets

app = Flask(__name__)
app.secret_key = os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY', os.urandom(32))

GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID = os.environ['GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID']
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET = os.environ['GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET']
GOOGLE_AUTH_URL = 'https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth'
GOOGLE_TOKEN_URL = 'https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token'
GOOGLE_USERINFO_URL = 'https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v2/userinfo'


@app.route('/api/auth/google')
def google_login():
    state = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    session['oauth_state'] = state

    params = {
        'client_id': GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID,
        'redirect_uri': url_for('google_callback', _external=True),
        'response_type': 'code',
        'scope': 'openid email profile',
        'state': state,
        'access_type': 'offline',
        'prompt': 'consent',
    }
    auth_url = f"{GOOGLE_AUTH_URL}?{'&'.join(f'{k}={v}' for k, v in params.items())}"
    return redirect(auth_url)


@app.route('/api/auth/google/callback')
def google_callback():
    # Verify state to prevent CSRF
    if request.args.get('state') != session.pop('oauth_state', None):
        return jsonify({'error': 'Invalid state parameter'}), 400

    if 'error' in request.args:
        return jsonify({'error': request.args['error']}), 400

    code = request.args.get('code')

    # Exchange code for tokens
    token_response = requests.post(GOOGLE_TOKEN_URL, data={
        'client_id': GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID,
        'client_secret': GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET,
        'code': code,
        'grant_type': 'authorization_code',
        'redirect_uri': url_for('google_callback', _external=True),
    })
    tokens = token_response.json()

    if 'error' in tokens:
        return jsonify({'error': 'Failed to exchange code'}), 400

    # Get user info
    headers = {'Authorization': f"Bearer {tokens['access_token']}"}
    user_response = requests.get(GOOGLE_USERINFO_URL, headers=headers)
    user_info = user_response.json()

    # Create or update user in your database
    user = find_or_create_user(
        provider='google',
        provider_id=user_info['id'],
        email=user_info['email'],
        name=user_info.get('name', ''),
        avatar=user_info.get('picture', ''),
    )

    session['user_id'] = user['id']
    session['email'] = user['email']

    return redirect('/dashboard')


def find_or_create_user(provider, provider_id, email, name, avatar):
    """Look up user by provider ID or email. Create if not found."""
    # In production, query your database here
    return {
        'id': 1,
        'email': email,
        'name': name,
        'avatar': avatar,
        'provider': provider,
    }

blocks.description: React component for Google sign-in button

blocks.title: Adding GitHub, Discord, and Other Providers

blocks.body: The OAuth 2.0 flow is the same for every provider -- only the URLs and scopes change. Create a reusable pattern by extracting provider configuration into a dictionary.

blocks.description: Multi-provider OAuth configuration -- add new providers by adding entries
blocks.code:
OAUTH_PROVIDERS = {
    'google': {
        'auth_url': 'https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth',
        'token_url': 'https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token',
        'userinfo_url': 'https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v2/userinfo',
        'scopes': 'openid email profile',
        'client_id': os.environ.get('GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID'),
        'client_secret': os.environ.get('GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET'),
        'id_field': 'id',
        'email_field': 'email',
        'name_field': 'name',
    },
    'github': {
        'auth_url': 'https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize',
        'token_url': 'https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token',
        'userinfo_url': 'https://api.github.com/user',
        'scopes': 'read:user user:email',
        'client_id': os.environ.get('GITHUB_CLIENT_ID'),
        'client_secret': os.environ.get('GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET'),
        'id_field': 'id',
        'email_field': 'email',
        'name_field': 'name',
    },
    'discord': {
        'auth_url': 'https://discord.com/api/oauth2/authorize',
        'token_url': 'https://discord.com/api/oauth2/token',
        'userinfo_url': 'https://discord.com/api/users/@me',
        'scopes': 'identify email',
        'client_id': os.environ.get('DISCORD_CLIENT_ID'),
        'client_secret': os.environ.get('DISCORD_CLIENT_SECRET'),
        'id_field': 'id',
        'email_field': 'email',
        'name_field': 'username',
    },
}


@app.route('/api/auth/<provider>')
def oauth_login(provider):
    config = OAUTH_PROVIDERS.get(provider)
    if not config:
        return jsonify({'error': f'Unknown provider: {provider}'}), 400

    state = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    session['oauth_state'] = state
    session['oauth_provider'] = provider

    params = {
        'client_id': config['client_id'],
        'redirect_uri': url_for('oauth_callback', provider=provider, _external=True),
        'response_type': 'code',
        'scope': config['scopes'],
        'state': state,
    }
    auth_url = f"{config['auth_url']}?{'&'.join(f'{k}={v}' for k, v in params.items())}"
    return redirect(auth_url)

blocks.heading: API Key Authentication
blocks.body: API keys are simple, static tokens used to identify and authorize API consumers. They are ideal for server-to-server communication, public APIs with rate limiting, and third-party integrations. Unlike JWTs, API keys do not encode user information -- they are opaque strings that the server looks up in a database.

blocks.description: Flask API key middleware with database lookup, rate limiting, and key management

blocks.title: API Key Security
blocks.body: API keys are like passwords -- never expose them in frontend code, URLs, or git repos. Always use environment variables and server-side requests. Prefix keys with your app name (e.g., myapp_sk_abc123) so they are easy to identify and can be detected by secret scanning tools.

blocks.heading: Basic Auth and Digest Auth
blocks.body: HTTP Basic Authentication is the simplest auth scheme defined in the HTTP standard. The client sends a header with the username and password encoded in base64 -- not encrypted, just encoded. Digest Auth improves on this by using a challenge-response mechanism where the password is hashed before sending. Both are rarely used in modern web apps but still appear in internal tools, APIs, and legacy systems.

blocks.description: Flask Basic Auth decorator for simple password-protected endpoints

blocks.title: Basic Auth Warning
blocks.body: Basic Auth sends credentials with EVERY request, and base64 encoding is trivially reversible -- it is NOT encryption. Only use Basic Auth over HTTPS. For production applications, prefer token-based auth (JWT) or session-based auth. Basic Auth is acceptable for internal tools, CI/CD webhooks, or quick prototypes.

blocks.heading: Passkeys and WebAuthn
blocks.body: Passkeys represent the future of authentication -- passwordless login using biometrics (fingerprint, face recognition) or hardware security keys. Built on the WebAuthn standard, passkeys use public-key cryptography: a private key stays on the user's device and never leaves it, while a public key is stored on your server. Authentication happens by signing a challenge with the private key, which the server verifies with the public key. No password to steal, phish, or forget.

blocks.description: Conceptual WebAuthn registration and authentication flow

blocks.heading: Passkey and WebAuthn Support
items: Chrome, Edge, Safari, and Firefox all support WebAuthn on desktop and mobile
iOS 16+ and Android 9+ support passkeys with cloud sync via iCloud Keychain and Google Password Manager
Windows Hello provides platform authenticator support (fingerprint, face, PIN)
Hardware keys like YubiKey work as cross-platform roaming authenticators
Passkeys can sync across devices within the same ecosystem (Apple, Google, Microsoft)
Cross-device authentication lets you use your phone's passkey to log into a desktop browser via QR code

blocks.heading: Magic Links
blocks.body: Magic links provide passwordless authentication through email. The user enters their email address, receives an email with a unique time-limited link, and clicking that link logs them in. No password to remember or type. This approach is used by Slack, Notion, and many modern SaaS apps for a frictionless login experience.

blocks.description: Flask magic link implementation with token generation, email sending, and verification
React component for requesting a magic link

blocks.heading: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA / 2FA)
blocks.body: Multi-factor authentication requires users to prove their identity using two or more independent factors. The three categories are: something you know (password, PIN), something you have (phone, hardware key), and something you are (fingerprint, face). By combining factors, even if an attacker steals your password, they cannot log in without the second factor.

cards.title: TOTP Apps
cards.body: Time-based one-time passwords generated by apps like Google Authenticator or Authy. Codes rotate every 30 seconds.

cards.title: SMS Codes
cards.body: One-time codes sent via text message. Convenient but vulnerable to SIM swapping attacks.

cards.title: Email Codes
cards.body: One-time codes sent to the user's email. Easy to implement but adds friction and depends on email security.

cards.title: Hardware Keys
cards.body: Physical USB or NFC devices like YubiKey. Most secure factor but requires purchasing hardware.

cards.title: Biometrics
cards.body: Fingerprint, face, or iris recognition. Convenient but requires device support and raises privacy concerns.

cards.title: Push Notifications
cards.body: Approve login from a trusted device with a single tap. Used by Duo, Microsoft Authenticator, and others.

blocks.description: Flask TOTP (authenticator app) implementation with setup and verification
blocks.code:
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify, session
import pyotp
import qrcode
import io
import base64

app = Flask(__name__)

# In production, store encrypted in database
user_totp_secrets = {}


@app.route('/api/mfa/setup', methods=['POST'])
def setup_totp():
    """Generate TOTP secret and QR code for authenticator app."""
    user_id = session.get('user_id')
    if not user_id:
        return jsonify({'error': 'Login required'}), 401

    # Generate a random secret
    secret = pyotp.random_base32()

    # Create provisioning URI for QR code
    totp = pyotp.TOTP(secret)
    provisioning_uri = totp.provisioning_uri(
        name=session.get('email', ''),
        issuer_name='MyApp'
    )

    # Generate QR code as base64 image
    qr = qrcode.QRCode(version=1, box_size=10, border=4)
    qr.add_data(provisioning_uri)
    qr.make(fit=True)
    img = qr.make_image(fill_color='black', back_color='white')

    buffer = io.BytesIO()
    img.save(buffer, format='PNG')
    qr_base64 = base64.b64encode(buffer.getvalue()).decode()

    # Store secret temporarily until verified
    session['pending_totp_secret'] = secret

    return jsonify({
        'secret': secret,  # Manual entry backup
        'qr_code': f'data:image/png;base64,{qr_base64}',
        'message': 'Scan this QR code with your authenticator app, then verify with a code.'
    })


@app.route('/api/mfa/verify-setup', methods=['POST'])
def verify_totp_setup():
    """Verify the user can generate valid codes before enabling MFA."""
    user_id = session.get('user_id')
    code = request.get_json().get('code', '')
    secret = session.get('pending_totp_secret')

    if not secret:
        return jsonify({'error': 'Start MFA setup first'}), 400

    totp = pyotp.TOTP(secret)
    if not totp.verify(code, valid_window=1):
        return jsonify({'error': 'Invalid code. Try again.'}), 400

    # Save the secret permanently
    user_totp_secrets[user_id] = secret
    session.pop('pending_totp_secret', None)

    return jsonify({'message': 'MFA enabled successfully'})


@app.route('/api/mfa/verify', methods=['POST'])
def verify_totp_login():
    """Verify TOTP code during login."""
    user_id = session.get('pending_mfa_user_id')
    code = request.get_json().get('code', '')

    if not user_id:
        return jsonify({'error': 'Complete primary login first'}), 400

    secret = user_totp_secrets.get(user_id)
    if not secret:
        return jsonify({'error': 'MFA not configured'}), 400

    totp = pyotp.TOTP(secret)
    if not totp.verify(code, valid_window=1):
        return jsonify({'error': 'Invalid code'}), 401

    # MFA passed -- complete login
    session.pop('pending_mfa_user_id', None)
    session['user_id'] = user_id
    session['mfa_verified'] = True

    return jsonify({'message': 'Login successful'})

blocks.title: SMS 2FA Weakness
blocks.body: SMS-based 2FA is better than no 2FA at all, but it is vulnerable to SIM swapping attacks where an attacker convinces your phone carrier to transfer your number to their SIM card. Prefer authenticator apps (TOTP) or hardware security keys. If you must support SMS, treat it as the weakest MFA option and encourage users to upgrade.

blocks.heading: Password Security
blocks.body: Passwords are still the most common authentication factor, and getting them wrong is one of the easiest ways to get hacked. The goal is simple: make it impossible for an attacker to recover passwords even if they steal your entire database. This means proper hashing, salting, and following modern password policies that prioritize length over complexity.

blocks.description: Proper password hashing with bcrypt -- registration and login verification

blocks.heading: Password Rules: DO vs DON'T
rows: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 for hashing
Use MD5 or SHA1 for password hashing
Limit password length to less than 64 characters
Truncate or silently modify user passwords
Use a unique salt per password (bcrypt does this)
Use a single global salt for all passwords
Lock accounts permanently after failed attempts

blocks.title: Critical Password Rule
blocks.body: NEVER store passwords in plaintext. NEVER use MD5 or SHA1 for password hashing -- they are too fast and can be brute-forced with modern GPUs. Use bcrypt (12+ rounds), scrypt, or argon2id with proper salt. Every major data breach that exposed passwords was made worse by weak hashing or plaintext storage.

blocks.heading: Authorization Patterns
blocks.body: Once you know who a user is (authentication), you need to decide what they are allowed to do (authorization). There are three main patterns, and most real applications use a combination of them.

blocks.heading: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
blocks.body: RBAC is the most common authorization model. Users are assigned roles (admin, editor, viewer), and each role has a set of permissions. When a user tries to do something, the system checks whether their role includes the required permission. RBAC is simple to understand, easy to implement, and works well for most applications.

blocks.description: Flask RBAC decorator with role hierarchy support

blocks.heading: Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
blocks.body: ABAC makes authorization decisions based on attributes of the user, the resource, the action, and the environment. Instead of broad roles, you write fine-grained policies like 'allow if user is in the same department as the document AND the current time is within business hours.' ABAC is more flexible than RBAC but also more complex to implement and audit.

blocks.description: Python ABAC policy engine with attribute-based rules

blocks.heading: RBAC vs ABAC vs Permission-Based
rows: Engineering dept can edit docs during business hours
User X has 'write' on Document Y

blocks.heading: Auth Architecture Patterns
blocks.body: How you structure authentication depends on your application architecture. A monolith handles everything in one place. Microservices need a centralized gateway or shared token validation. And sometimes the best move is to outsource auth entirely to a specialized service.

In a monolith, authentication and authorization live inside the same application as your business logic. The server handles login, token/session management, and permission checks all in one process. This is the simplest architecture and works well for most applications.

blocks.description: Monolith auth architecture -- everything in one server
blocks.code:
Client (Browser/Mobile)
    |
    |  POST /api/login  { email, password }
    v
+------------------------------------------+
|           Monolith Server                |
|                                          |
|  [Auth Module]                           |
|    - Login / Register                    |
|    - Session or JWT management           |
|    - Password hashing                    |
|                                          |
|  [Middleware]                            |
|    - Verify session/token on each req    |
|    - Attach user to request context      |
|                                          |
|  [Business Logic]                        |
|    - Check permissions inline            |
|    - @require_role('admin')              |
|                                          |
|  [Database]                              |
|    - users, sessions, roles tables       |
+------------------------------------------+

blocks.body: In a microservices architecture, an API Gateway sits in front of all services and handles authentication centrally. The gateway verifies JWTs, and downstream services trust the gateway's forwarded headers. This avoids duplicating auth logic across every service.

blocks.description: Microservice auth with API Gateway pattern
blocks.code:
Client (Browser/Mobile)
    |
    |  Authorization: Bearer <JWT>
    v
+---------------------+
|    API Gateway      |
|  - Verify JWT       |
|  - Rate limiting    |
|  - Route requests   |
+---------------------+
    |           |           |
    | X-User-Id | X-User-Id | X-User-Id
    | X-Role    | X-Role    | X-Role
    v           v           v
+--------+ +--------+ +--------+
| Users  | | Posts  | | Orders |
| Service| | Service| | Service|
+--------+ +--------+ +--------+
    |           |           |
    v           v           v
  [DB]        [DB]        [DB]

Auth Service (separate)
  - POST /auth/login -> returns JWT
  - POST /auth/register
  - POST /auth/refresh
  - GET  /auth/verify -> validates token

blocks.body: Auth-as-a-Service providers handle the entire authentication flow for you: login pages, social login, MFA, password resets, user management, and compliance. Your application just verifies tokens issued by the provider. This is the fastest way to add production-grade auth but gives you less control and adds a vendor dependency.

blocks.description: Auth-as-a-Service architecture with a third-party provider
blocks.code:
Client (Browser/Mobile)
    |
    |  1. Redirect to Auth Provider login page
    v
+------------------------+
| Auth Provider          |
| (Auth0 / Clerk /       |
|  Supabase / Firebase)  |
|                        |
| - Hosted login page    |
| - Social login (Google,|
|   GitHub, etc.)        |
| - MFA enrollment       |
| - Password reset       |
| - User management      |
+------------------------+
    |
    |  2. Returns JWT (ID token + access token)
    v
Client
    |
    |  3. Authorization: Bearer <JWT>
    v
+----------------------------+
| Your Backend               |
| - Verify JWT signature     |
|   using provider's JWKS    |
| - Extract user claims      |
| - Check permissions/roles  |
| - Business logic only      |
+----------------------------+

blocks.heading: Security Checklist
blocks.body: Auth is only as strong as its weakest link. Use this checklist to audit your authentication and authorization implementation. Every item matters -- attackers look for the one thing you forgot.

blocks.heading: Complete Auth Security Checklist
items: Enforce HTTPS everywhere -- never send credentials over plain HTTP. Use HSTS headers to prevent downgrade attacks.
Hash passwords with bcrypt (12+ rounds), scrypt, or argon2id. Never use MD5, SHA1, or SHA256 for passwords.
Set secure cookie flags: HttpOnly (no JavaScript access), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite=Lax or Strict (CSRF protection).
Implement CSRF protection for session-based auth -- use CSRF tokens or SameSite cookies.
Configure CORS properly -- never use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials. Whitelist specific origins.
Rate-limit login endpoints -- max 5-10 attempts per minute per IP/account. Use exponential backoff.
Implement account lockout after repeated failures -- lock for 15-30 minutes, notify the user by email.
Use short-lived access tokens (15 minutes) with longer-lived refresh tokens (7 days) stored in httpOnly cookies.
Validate and sanitize all auth inputs -- prevent SQL injection in login queries and XSS in error messages.
Return generic error messages -- say 'Invalid email or password' not 'No account with that email' (prevents enumeration).
Implement proper logout -- revoke tokens, clear sessions, delete cookies on both client and server.
Log all authentication events -- successful logins, failed attempts, password changes, MFA enrollments, token refreshes.
Rotate secrets and keys regularly -- JWT signing keys, API keys, OAuth client secrets.
Validate redirect URIs in OAuth flows -- use exact matching, never allow open redirects.
Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent XSS attacks that could steal tokens.
Use timing-safe comparison for token and password verification to prevent timing attacks.
Set appropriate token and session expiration times -- balance security with user experience.
Implement password reset securely -- use time-limited tokens, send to verified email only, invalidate after use.

blocks.title: Security Confidence
blocks.body: If you implement everything in this checklist, your auth system will be more secure than 90% of web applications. Most breaches happen because of skipped basics, not sophisticated attacks. Focus on getting the fundamentals right before adding advanced features.

blocks.heading: Common Auth Vulnerabilities
blocks.body: Understanding how attacks work is essential for building secure auth. Here are the most common vulnerabilities, how they are exploited, and how to prevent them.

blocks.heading: Auth Vulnerabilities and Defenses
rows: Attacker enters ' OR 1=1 -- as username to bypass login
Use parameterized queries or an ORM. Never concatenate user input into SQL.
Attacker sets a known session ID before victim logs in, then hijacks the session
Regenerate session ID after login. Never accept session IDs from URL parameters.
Malicious site submits forms to your app using the victim's cookies
Use CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, and verify Origin/Referer headers.
Injected script reads JWT from localStorage or document.cookie
Store tokens in httpOnly cookies. Use Content Security Policy headers. Sanitize output.
Attacker tries thousands of password combinations against a login endpoint
Rate limiting, account lockout, CAPTCHA after failed attempts, strong password requirements.
Attacker uses leaked email/password combos from other breaches to try your site
Check passwords against breached lists (HIBP), enforce MFA, detect unusual login patterns.
Attacker requests password reset and intercepts or guesses the reset token
Use long random tokens with short expiry. Send only to verified email. One-time use. Invalidate on password change.
Attacker changes JWT header from RS256 to HS256, signs with the public key as HMAC secret
Always specify allowed algorithms in jwt.decode(). Never use 'none' algorithm. Pin the expected algorithm.
